title: Founded at Pike Place Market
description: Jerry Baldwin, Zev Siegl and Gordon Bowker open the first Starbucks in Seattle, selling roasted whole-bean coffee — no espresso drinks yet. The name comes from Moby-Dick.

title: Howard Schultz joins
description: Schultz is hired as director of retail operations and marketing, running a company that is still just a handful of Seattle bean shops.

title: The Milan trip
description: A buying-trip stop in Milan exposes Schultz to Italian espresso-bar culture and the idea of the cafe as a social gathering place — the seed of the 'third place' concept.

title: Il Giornale, then the buyout
description: Unable to convince the founders to sell coffee drinks, Schultz leaves to start his own espresso-bar chain, Il Giornale. In 1987 he uses it, backed by investors, to buy Starbucks outright and becomes CEO.

title: From 11 to 165 stores
description: Under Schultz, the company grows from 11 stores and $1.2M revenue (1987) past 100 stores within four years.

title: IPO on NASDAQ
description: Starbucks goes public, raising roughly $40M and funding a decade of aggressive, capital-intensive expansion instead of franchising it out.

title: Frappuccino launches
description: The blended iced drink becomes one of the biggest product-driven revenue expansions in company history.

title: First stores outside North America
description: Starbucks opens in Tokyo (Ginza) and Singapore, crossing 1,000 total stores the same year.

title: Enters the United Kingdom
description: Starbucks acquires Seattle Coffee Company to enter the UK market.

title: Enters mainland China
description: First China store opens at the China World Trade Center in Beijing — the beginning of what becomes Starbucks' second-largest market.

title: Schultz steps back
description: Schultz hands the CEO role to Orin Smith and becomes chairman, staying close to strategy as international expansion accelerates.

title: C.A.F.E. Practices & first Farmer Support Center
description: Starbucks launches its ethical-sourcing verification program with SCS Global Services and opens its first Farmer Support Center in San José, Costa Rica.

title: 10,000 stores
description: Starbucks crosses the 10,000-store mark globally, capping a decade of rapid, sometimes over-aggressive, expansion.

title: Schultz returns amid crisis
description: Facing falling profit and an over-expanded store base, Schultz returns as CEO. Starbucks closes roughly 600 underperforming U.S. stores and, in a widely covered move, shuts 7,100 U.S. stores for three hours on February 26 to retrain baristas on espresso quality.

title: Deeper cuts, then VIA
description: 300 more stores close and thousands of jobs are cut as the financial crisis peaks; Starbucks stock bottoms out near $2.85 before recovering. The company launches VIA Instant to reach budget-conscious, at-home coffee drinkers.

title: Logo simplifies, mobile begins
description: Starbucks drops the words 'Starbucks Coffee' from its logo, keeping only the siren, and launches one of the retail industry's earliest mobile-payment apps.

title: First Reserve Roastery
description: Starbucks opens its first Reserve Roastery in Seattle — an experiential, small-batch format that later expands to Shanghai, Milan, New York, Tokyo and Chicago.

title: Full ownership of China
description: Starbucks buys out its East China joint-venture partners for $1.3B, taking full ownership of mainland China operations it had run jointly since entry — a bet on China as its next growth engine.

title: Nestlé deal, Philadelphia incident, Italy entry
description: Starbucks sells global marketing rights for its packaged/retail coffee to Nestlé for $7.15B; closes 8,000+ U.S. stores for an afternoon of company-wide racial-bias training after a widely publicized incident in a Philadelphia store; and finally enters Italy, the birthplace of the espresso culture that inspired the company.

title: Pandemic pivot
description: COVID-19 forces temporary closures and an accelerated shift toward drive-thru, curbside and mobile-order-only 'Pickup' store formats.

title: Unionization begins
description: Baristas at two Buffalo, NY stores vote to unionize in December, founding what becomes Starbucks Workers United — the start of a labor movement that spreads to hundreds of U.S. stores.

title: Schultz returns again, exits Russia
description: Schultz comes back as interim CEO for a third time amid union pushback and slowing growth. Starbucks exits Russia after the invasion of Ukraine; the ~130 stores reopen months later, locally owned, as 'Stars Coffee.'

title: New CEO, new values
description: Laxman Narasimhan takes over as CEO. Starbucks introduces five core values — Craft, Results, Courage, Belonging, Joy — developed with input from hundreds of partners.

title: Leadership change amid decline
description: Persistent sales declines and activist-investor pressure (Elliott Management, Starboard) lead the board to replace Narasimhan in August. Former Chipotle CEO Brian Niccol is named Chairman & CEO in September.

title: "Back to Starbucks"
description: Niccol launches a turnaround plan: a simplified menu, condiment bars and ceramic mugs return, a $1B restructuring closes 627 stores and cuts corporate roles, and a new mission statement is issued in January. A nationwide Workers United strike runs November 13, 2025 through early April 2026. In November, Starbucks sells 60% of its China business to Boyu Capital in a $4B joint venture. Q4 FY2025 delivers the first positive global comparable sales in eight quarters.

title: Turnaround gains speed
description: Contract talks with the union resume in April after 131 days on strike. Global comparable sales accelerate to +4% (Q1) and +6.2% (Q2), led by a 7.1% jump in North America, as the store-remodel and menu-simplification program continues.
